title: Pre-Decide Tomorrow's Three Big Decisions
subtitle: A five-minute evening activity for reducing decision fatigue before it starts
excerpt: A five-minute evening activity for reducing tomorrow's decision load before it arrives. Each evening, decide three things in advance: what starts first, what gets dropped or delegated, and what constitutes done enough for the day.
sourceNotes: Draws on decision fatigue research and the established practice of evening planning to reduce morning cognitive load.

practice.whenToUse: Use each evening as a brief planning practice. Particularly useful on days when the morning starts pressured or when decision-making feels heavy.

content.text: A five-minute evening planning practice for reducing decision fatigue. Decision fatigue is cumulative: the more decisions made earlier in the day, the worse the later ones tend to be. Pre-deciding the three decisions that cost the most reduces the load before the day starts.
New to activities as a practice? Read
Each evening, answer three questions about tomorrow. Write the answers down. Do not leave them as intentions:
What gets done first? Not what is most urgent. What task, if done first, makes the rest of the day easier or less fraught?

What gets dropped or delegated? One item from tomorrow's list that will either not happen or will be handed to someone else. Naming it in advance means you are not spending tomorrow's attention deciding whether to drop it.
What does done enough look like today? A single sentence describing what tomorrow needs to contain for you to close the day as a reasonable one. Not a perfect day. A done-enough day.
The three answers are pre-made decisions. They do not all have to hold once the day starts. But having them written means you begin from a position rather than from scratch.
Draws on decision fatigue research and evening planning principles.
